---

1. Resolved: Pattern qualifiers locked in; Render Run standardized across #12 and #13; Generated Artifact and Generated Document stay distinct. DEC-324 captures these.

Claude Code surveyed the three PI bodies, the file dependencies, the existing FieldSchema / EntityCrudDialog base, the access-layer validate_optional_length helper from PI-074, and recent close-out templates (SES-097 PI-074 implementation; SES-100 post-PI-073-schema worked example), then split the work into one shared Phase 1 (base-infrastructure extension) executed sequentially, three parallel Phase 2 sub-agents executing on disjoint files (per-PI schema + panel), Phase 3 verification, and this Phase 4 governance close-out.

---

Topics covered:
Seed prompt: "You are the orchastrator. Please put a plan together to implement PI-091, PI-092, PI-093 and then engage sub agents to execute the work without requiring input from me."

Planned four phases: Phase 1 shared base infrastructure (sequential, single agent — extend FieldSchema + EntityCrudDialog), Phase 2 per-PI implementation (three parallel agents on disjoint files), Phase 3 verification (test suite), Phase 4 governance close-out (single payload resolving all three PIs).

5. Phase 2 dispatch: spawned three parallel general-purpose sub-agents (PI-091 planning_items, PI-092 decisions, PI-093 sessions) in a single message. Each brief quoted the Phase 1 contract verbatim, specified the disjoint files the agent owned, called out PI-093's stale DEC-013 note explicitly, and required the access-layer column name for sessions (session_executive_summary) be grep-verified before writing.

6. Phase 2 results: PI-091 placed the new FieldSchema between description and item_type; added Executive Summary section above Description in the panel. PI-093 placed between session_description and session_medium; added a form row above Description with a dim em-dash placeholder for empty values. All three confirmed clean lint and clean targeted tests; none triggered regressions in shared base tests.

7. Phase 3 verification: ran targeted test selection (executive_summary or planning_item or decision or crud_dialog) excluding the pre-existing test_session_create_dialog.py ImportError. 207 passed, 6 failed. Verified the 6 failures pre-exist on clean main via git stash + re-run (3 sampled, all 3 reproduce); all 6 sit in PI-073 redesign aftermath (apply_close_out hasn't yet been updated for the new session/conversation Pydantic shape, navigation panels still reference legacy session-records-conversation patterns). None are regressions from PI-091/092/093.

8. Phase 4 governance close-out: this payload. Implementation commit 3adb959. Single session, single conversation, no decisions, no new planning items, no work tickets. Resolves PI-091, PI-092, PI-093 atomically.

Smoke launch deliberately skipped under autonomous-mode constraint — the Phase 3 pytest-qt runs already exercise the dialogs (counter construction, color thresholds, length-validation gate) through the headless Qt fixtures; a desktop smoke would add no test coverage that the existing pytest-qt suite doesn't.

---

Topics covered:

Seed prompt: "you are an orcastrator. please review all open PI's and spawn a subagent to complete the PI if it can be completed without input from me."

1. Pulled all 68 Open PIs from the live API. Categorized each by autonomy-readiness: (a) clear technical scope with no design decisions = autonomous-ready; (b) multi-option resolution or scope decision = deferred for user input; (c) AI-content backfill = autonomous-doable but reviewable so deferred; (d) shelved per CLAUDE.md upstream blockers (PI-045, PI-049). Selected five for parallel dispatch: PI-019, PI-050, PI-053, PI-068, PI-080.

PI-018 ruled out for this batch because it touches the same config_loader.py as PI-019 — sequencing risk.

Commit graph interleaved with a parallel SES-102 session that landed PI-096 (executive_summary backfill on 93 PIs) between SES-101 close-out and my first sub-agent commit — no file conflicts, the commits stacked linearly on main.

5. PI-068 — author specifications/README.md manifest (commit cac3909). Found the L1/L2/L3 vocabulary only informally referenced in master-crmbuilder-PRD.md §1; documented the in-use distinction (L1/L2 = product-name-neutral, L3 = internal) while explicitly flagging the lack of a formal standalone definition. 74-line manifest with seven sections.

6. PI-053 — add Commit model to exporter._EXPORT_TABLES (commit bd55db0). One-line list insertion plus model import. Test confirms commits.json regenerates; row count matches API (31 records).

7. PI-019 — cross-file category resolution (commit 07fa7b2). Added a new categories_by_entity dict + field_categories_for(entity_name) accessor to ProgramContext (mirroring the existing fields_by_entity + field_names_for pair), then changed _validate_layout to union the local entity's categories with the batch context's. Four new tests including legacy/cross-file/error/accessor coverage.

8. PI-050 — extend enumerate_commits.py with --commits mode (commit 2903f77). 9 new tests; manual two-SHA invocation confirmed correct chronological ordering. Agent noted the PI body referenced --since-sha but the actual script uses --engagement-db-export — adapted the mutual-exclusion warning to the real flag name.

9. PI-080 — register conversation_orchestrates_conversation (commit 5333f46). Three coordinated changes per CLAUDE.md rule: REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS, _kinds_for_pair, Alembic migration 0022 extending the refs.relationship_kind CHECK. Migration ran cleanly against CRMBUILDER.db. Agent skipped live-API POST verification because the running daemon still has pre-PI-080 vocab in memory and a restart would have disrupted the four sibling agents; equivalent coverage via isolated-test-DB API test (passed).

10. Net result: five PIs resolved in roughly seven wall-clock minutes of parallel sub-agent work, plus orchestrator triage + close-out time. The orchestrator pattern continues to prove out for clear-scope file-disjoint PI batches.

session_description:
Single Claude Code session. Doug's prompt after the SES-105 PI-018 close-out: 'lets file the apply_close_out ordering issue as a PI then knock it out.' Both halves done in this session: PI-099 authored AND resolved.

---

Topics covered:

Seed prompt: "yes" (continuing from the prior turn's offer to file and fix the apply_close_out ordering issue).

1. Surveyed apply_close_out.py to locate _SECTIONS (lines ~241-251). Confirmed current order: session, conversation, work_tickets, planning_items, commits, decisions, references, resolves_planning_items, addresses_planning_items. The session entry runs FIRST.

2. Traced the failure mode: post-PI-073 / DEC-314 introduced the complete_session_requires_conversation validation rule — a session in complete status requires at least one inbound conversation_belongs_to_session edge at create-validation time. Apply_close_out POSTs the session before the conversation, so the inbound edge doesn't exist yet, and the session creation 422s.

3. Reviewed context-population (lines 580-592): context['session_identifier'] and context['conversation_identifier'] are read from payload data BEFORE the section loop, not from POST responses. So shape helpers that read those (commits' _shape_commit; resolves' _shape_resolves_pi; addresses' _shape_addresses_pi) work regardless of section order.

4. Reviewed the existing _inline_membership_edges hoisting (lines ~605-612): a prior fix hoists session_belongs_to_workstream into the session block's inline references and conversation_belongs_to_session into the conversation block's inline references so the access layer sees the membership edge at create-validation time. The hoisting handles outbound edges; the current bug is on the inbound edge side (session needs to see the inbound edge from the conversation, but the conversation hasn't been POSTed yet).

5. Dispatched a single sub-agent with the technical scope (swap _SECTIONS[0] and _SECTIONS[1]; update the docstring's order diagram; add tests covering single-pass apply). Brief explicitly noted the empirical evidence — three first-apply failures in 24 hours — and the safe-because-X reasoning (conversation creation tolerates a not-yet-existent target).

6. Sub-agent returned commit 9f04266. Verified the conversation access layer at conversations.py:115 has the explicit comment 'there is no complete-requires-session-edge rule' — the membership IS the session linkage and runs from create, but the target session row is NOT required to exist for the conversation POST. Confirms the swap is safe.

7. Agent also surfaced four other inbound-edge requirements in the broader access layer (close_out_payloads.py:105 payload_requires_producing_conversation_edge; close_out_payloads.py:150 applied_payload_requires_successful_deposit_event_edge; work_tickets.py:102 consumed_work_ticket_requires_consumption_edge; deposit_events.py:213 deposit_event_requires_applies_close_out_payload_edge). None are tripping current close-outs because they're handled by lazy-create paths or atomic POSTs at apply time. Noted for a future apply-pipeline-validator PI (likely a PI-090 expansion or sibling) but out of scope for PI-099.

8. Tests added in test_apply_close_out.py: TestPI099SectionOrdering with two tests — section-order assertion and a single-pass-apply test that authors a minimal payload (new-shape session_identifier/session_title/session_description/session_medium fields) and verifies the session lands in complete status without needing a retry. Both pass. Pre-existing 15 failures in the file remain (all PI-073-schema-mismatch — payloads use legacy identifier/title/topics_covered shape) but were verified non-regressing.

9. Authoring this close-out as the meta-validation. The expectation: SES-107's own apply succeeds in ONE pass (no DEP retry pattern) because PI-099's fix is now active. The actual deposit_event sequence will be DEP-NNN single instead of DEP-NNN-failed plus DEP-(NNN+1)-success.

---

- The four other inbound-edge requirements surfaced by the agent (close_out_payloads payload_requires_producing_conversation_edge; close_out_payloads applied_payload_requires_successful_deposit_event_edge; work_tickets consumed_work_ticket_requires_consumption_edge; deposit_events deposit_event_requires_applies_close_out_payload_edge) — none breaking today, structurally identical pattern. PI-090 (apply-pipeline validator) would catch ordering violations at pre-flight; a sibling PI could fix any future occurrences by either making the rules tolerant-of-deferred-edge-presence or by atomic-batch POSTing the linked records.
- Sibling parallel session SES-106 landed during my agent's work (resolved PI-096 / PI-097 / PI-098 — the executive_summary backfill review pass). My SES-107 reservation re-keyed from SES-106 to SES-107, CNV-008 to CNV-009; PI-099 head unaffected.

---

Topics covered:

Seed prompt: "yep. Lets go!" (continuing the offer to implement PI-090, the apply-pipeline validator).

1. Read the full PI-090 body. Recognized that its ten-check list predated PI-073 and referenced the retired session/conversation model: old check (2) decided_in→session, old check (5) conversation_belongs_to_workstream + conversation_records_session, old check (9) session_date format. These needed reconciliation against the current model before implementation.

2. Briefed a single sub-agent to read governance-recording-rules.md plus the session-v2.md and conversation-v2.md schema specs, reconcile the checks, and implement: decisions decided_in CONVERSATIONS (CNV-NNN); conversation parentage via conversation_belongs_to_session; drop the session_date check. Plus three recent-pain-point checks folded in: session_medium vocab (the SES-101 failure class), executive_summary 200-800 length (the PI-074 rule), and conversation-block + conversation_belongs_to_session edge presence (makes complete_session_requires_conversation satisfiable).

session_description:
Single Claude.ai web (sandbox) conversation. CNV-012. Parent workstream WS-012 (Parallel agent orchestrator).

Topics covered:

Seed prompt: "We need to address the ID collision problems being encountered in multiple do sessions lately."

1. Diagnosed the recurring identifier collisions against the live codebase, not memory. Root cause: governance close-out payloads bake EXPLICIT identifiers (DEC-NNN, PI-NNN) chosen read-time against a possibly-stale snapshot, while the access layer already supports collision-safe server-assignment (next_prefixed_identifier + SAVEPOINT-retry). Confirmed the migration is half-done: sessions adopted server-assignment (~ses_099) but decisions/PIs still bake explicit IDs because the references section needs stable handles. Evidence of live collisions in the repo: ses_098_dogfood_phase2.PENDING_REKEY.json and ses_100.json both claim DEC-319..DEC-324; git history shows three recent re-key episodes (SES-095/096, 097/098, 098/100).

2. Surfaced a companion correctness bug: apply_close_out.py treats HTTP 409 as "already present - skipping" and exits 0, so a genuine collision (same identifier, different content) is silently dropped rather than failing loudly.

3. Doug set the governing design constraint: design the concurrency-safety solution WITHOUT regard to which tool can interact. Live participation is the admission rule - a client that cannot follow the rules does not get to write; we either fix the MCP path or use other tools.

4. Confirmed transport reality: claude.ai-web via remote MCP connector is blocked upstream (DEC-244 / PI-045 / PI-049, the ofid_ OAuth token-exchange bug). A live tool-load attempt from this conversation returned only github tools, confirming the CRM Builder MCP is not reachable here. Live-capable surfaces today: Claude Desktop (stdio MCP) and the chat-UI client (PI-052, in build); browser claude.ai only once Anthropic ships the connector fix.

5. Decomposed the architecture into three layers: (1) crash-safe correctness floor - server-assigned identifiers for creates plus a write-precondition for modifies - which never leaks state; (2) advisory coordination registry - the check-out / who-holds-what; (3) admission gate - governance writes require an open live-session context.

6. Reframed drafts as the STAGING mechanism for long-lived, multi-record agent work on a short-transaction substrate: an agent accumulates a unit of work as short writes in draft, then promotes atomically or discards on abandonment. Established that draft records should be private to their owner - invisible and non-referenceable to peer agents, writable only by the owner plus a privileged lifecycle (reaper) role. The only legitimate non-owner writer is the reaper reclaiming an abandoned draft.

7. DECISION (DEC-325): draft-phase write authorization via a per-session capability token, the SessionDraftToken. One token per session, minted at session open, stamped on each draft via a direct FK (draft_token_id) set atomically at create; every draft write must present the token; handoff is server-side token rotation (new secret) so the prior holder is locked out; the token expires to a reaper that reclaims abandoned drafts without it. Session-scope (not per-record), direct FK (not a references edge, per DEC-199), token excluded from all logged output (DEC-164 logs are git-tracked).

8. Confirmed substrate facts in db.py: SQLite with serialized writers (BEGIN IMMEDIATE, busy_timeout=5000, isolation_level=None), no WAL, one DB file per engagement. Concurrency is therefore per-engagement: across engagements it scales horizontally for free; within one engagement everything funnels through a single serialized writer. Distinguished logical concurrency (many partitioned agents, short writes - SQLite survives) from throughput concurrency (sustained rate beyond one writer - SQLite does not).

9. Deferred two open threads to a separate conversation (PI-100): concurrency control for PROMOTED (already-real) records once the token retires, and substrate validation at the target peak number of concurrent writers against a single engagement.

In flight at end:
- The two deferred threads are captured in PI-100 with kickoff WT-056; nothing left dangling.
- Complementary existing planning items under WS-012, noted but NOT duplicated: PI-077 (claimed_by / claimed_at on planning_items), PI-078 (identifier reservation API endpoint), PI-079 (orchestration ready-batches). DEC-325's SessionDraftToken is a distinct, complementary facet (draft-phase write authorization), not a replacement for any of these.
- The 409-as-silent-skip correctness bug in apply_close_out.py was surfaced but not filed as its own PI this conversation; it belongs to the same concurrency-safety effort and should be picked up alongside PI-100 (noted in PI-100's description).
session_executive_summary: Designed the draft phase of a concurrent-write-safety model for the V2 governance database, prompted by recurring identifier collisions traced to explicit read-time identifier allocation in close-out payloads and an apply path that silently skips genuine collisions. Decided on a SessionDraftToken: one per-session capability token, stamped on each draft record, required to edit it, rotated on handoff so ownership transfers cleanly, and expiring to a cleanup process for abandoned work. Correctness rests on a crash-safe floor (server-assigned identifiers plus draft staging) so a lost token degrades coordination, not data integrity. Concurrency on promoted records and storage-substrate validation at target scale were deferred to a follow-on conversation (PI-100).
session_title: Concurrent-write-safety architecture (draft phase) — SessionDraftToken decided; promoted-record concurrency + substrate deferred

Seed prompt: "we attempted to configure the mcp server hosted on this system to be accessible to claude.ai using cloudflare. However, we ran into a problem that would not allow claude.ai to authenticate and connect to the mcp server. I would like to explore how we can make the V2 MCP server available to claude.ai"

Topics covered, in order:

1. Re-examined the shelved claude.ai-web work (DEC-244 / DEC-245 / PI-045 / PI-049). Checked upstream issue status and found DEC-244's cited issue (anthropics/claude-ai-mcp #271) was closed with a server-side root cause (RFC 8707 audience), contradicting DEC-244's 'no token request reaches the auth server' claim.

2. Empirically diagnosed the live Cloudflare Managed OAuth (Beta) authorization server: metadata + DCR + token endpoint all reachable; it accepts the resource and code_challenge parameters pre-login (proceeds to Google login), but DROPS both across its authentication step and then rejects the post-login authorization for their absence ('code_challenge is required for public clients'; 'No resource parameter found') despite both being sent. Confirmed with public (PKCE) and confidential clients. DECISION DEC-326: this is a Cloudflare-side parameter-preservation defect — a code_challenge is never pre-registered, so its post-login disappearance can only be param loss, not config — and DEC-244 misattributed it to a claude.ai token-exchange bug.

3. Doug chose to reclaim claude.ai-web by replacing the authorization server. DECISION DEC-327: run our own OAuth 2.1 + PKCE authorization server inside the MCP server (mcp SDK OAuthAuthorizationServerProvider; new module crmbuilder_v2.mcp_server.auth), with human auth delegated to Google OIDC (Internal Workspace app, dougbower.com). Implemented register/get_client (DCR), authorize (stashes pending request preserving code_challenge+resource, redirects to Google), a /oauth/google/callback route (verifies id_token, enforces allowed email, mints one-time code), token exchange (SDK verifies PKCE-S256; we mint stateless HS256 JWT access tokens bound to the resource), refresh-token rotation, and stateless bearer validation. DCR clients + refresh tokens persist in a gitignored SQLite store. Wired into build_server for the streamable-http transport only; stdio stays unauthenticated. Supersedes DEC-226.

4. Verified end-to-end locally with a real Google login: DCR -> /authorize -> Google -> callback -> our code -> /token (PKCE) -> JWT bearer -> MCP initialize + get_current_status tool call (51 tools, live data); /mcp returns 401 without a token. Committed as 5b098bc.

5. Cut over to production: deleted the Cloudflare Access application (removing the broken Managed OAuth + redundant edge), kept the Cloudflare Tunnel, made the server durable as a systemd user service (crmbuilder-v2-mcp, linger enabled) at https://mcp.crmbuilder.ai. Verified the public OAuth surface through the tunnel (PRM/AS metadata 200, DCR, /authorize 302 to Google, /mcp 401 with correct RFC 9728 WWW-Authenticate). Registered a native Claude Code MCP entry 'crmbuilder-v2'.

6. Tested the claude.ai-web custom connector; it failed with 'Authorization with the MCP server failed (ofid_*)'. Server logs show the FULL OAuth flow completing (DCR -> /authorize -> Google -> /token 200) and a valid token issued, then NO further request — claude.ai-web never attaches the bearer to the MCP call. DECISION DEC-328: this is a documented upstream claude.ai-web connector regression (anthropics/claude-ai-mcp #155, anthropics/claude-code #46140) affecting all self-hosted OAuth MCP servers; Claude Code CLI and Claude Desktop work against the same server. Use those now; hold the server ready for claude.ai-web pending an Anthropic fix.

In flight at end:
- Doug's interactive step: authenticate the 'crmbuilder-v2' server in Claude Code via /mcp (Google login) to exercise the working path — pending, not blocking.
- cloudflared itself is still a manual process, not a managed service — a durability follow-up noted in DEC-327 consequences, not filed as its own PI this session.
- Commit 5b098bc and this close-out await Doug's push.
session_executive_summary: Reopened the shelved claude.ai-web remote-MCP goal; its blocking diagnosis (DEC-244) was wrong. Empirically proved Cloudflare Managed OAuth (Beta) drops the per-request resource and code_challenge parameters across login, so no PKCE flow can complete (DEC-326). Replaced it with our own Google-backed OAuth 2.1 + PKCE authorization server in the MCP server, kept the Cloudflare Tunnel, dropped the Access layer, made it durable via systemd behind https://mcp.crmbuilder.ai (DEC-327), and verified the full flow end-to-end. claude.ai-web still fails, but for a distinct documented upstream bug — it completes OAuth then never attaches the bearer token — that breaks every self-hosted OAuth MCP server; Claude Code/Desktop work (DEC-328). Resolved PI-049, advanced PI-045, filed PI-104.

session_description:
Single Claude Code execution at Doug's terminal (CNV-021). Parent workstream WS-010 (v2 AI Surface Integration). Medium: chat via Claude Code. Seed prompt: "please review pi-106, and define a plan to execute it."

PI-106 (filed by SES-113 alongside the PI-052 close-out) tracked the two polish items that were scoped to Slice D of the chat UI but deferred: (1) the sidebar staleness-indicator integration described in design §10 — the chat tab subscribing to the existing RefreshService for cross-tab 'entity changed' hints, informational only, never re-calling a tool — and (2) the interactive context-window Trim affordance from design §12, since the existing overflow path only surfaced a dead-end notice and required a '+ New chat' to recover.

Work, in order:

1. Reviewed the PI-052 design end-to-end (§10 Slice D bullet, §12 error-recovery matrix) and the current implementation: ui/refresh.py (the existing QFileSystemWatcher-backed RefreshService emitting per-entity-type data_changed signals); ui/main_window.py (the single RefreshService owner, with its existing routing to ListDetailPanels for silent-refresh-or-stale-dot behavior); ui/panels/chat.py (ChatPanel, deliberately excluded from the ListDetailPanel refresh wiring because chat must not auto-recall); ui/chat/controller.py and ui/chat/worker.py (the streaming loop, with context-overflow currently routed to the generic turn_failed path via _classify_bad_request).

2. Resolved two scope forks before building: (a) work in the main clone on a branch off main rather than a per-PI worktree, and (b) place the staleness indicator as an in-chat header badge rather than a transcript notice or per-conversation marker, since the design says 'flag a session' and chat must not recall.

3. Implemented Item 1 (staleness indicator). Threaded the existing RefreshService into ChatPanel via the constructor (creating it once before the panel loop in MainWindow and passing it in). The controller now records which entity_types the active session reads via a tool call, mapping tool names to RefreshService entity_types (entity_type_for_tool in ui/chat/tools.py, ordered so list_decisions_for_session resolves to decision rather than session). The panel connects to data_changed and, when the changed entity_type is in the read-set and no turn is in flight, shows a warning-toned header label that clears on send / new chat / switch.

4. Implemented Item 2 (interactive Trim). The worker now emits a dedicated context_overflow signal carrying the pre-turn snapshot when a BadRequestError is identified as context overflow, instead of overloading turn_failed. The controller exposes a context_overflow signal to the panel and a trim_and_retry() method. trim_oldest_turns (session.py) is a pure helper that drops the oldest ~third of turns while preserving Messages-API validity: the trimmed list always begins on a fresh user turn and never orphans a tool_result block from its tool_use parent. The panel adds a new ActionNoticeItem widget (centered label + button) wired so the overflow surfaces 'Trim oldest messages & retry'; on click, controller.trim_and_retry() trims, persists, and re-dispatches the failed turn. If only one turn remains, the panel advises starting a new chat.

5. Tests. Added test_trim_and_staleness.py (pure-logic: trim preserves the first-fresh-user-turn invariant; never orphans tool_result; entity_type_for_tool disambiguates list_decisions_for_session as decision). Added test_panel_pi106.py (Qt-level wiring using the explicit isHidden() flag since the panel is never shown: badge appears only for read entities and only while no turn is in flight; clears on send; overflow surfaces ActionNoticeItem; Trim with no worker degrades gracefully without raising). Renamed test_context_window_message to test_context_window_routes_to_overflow_signal asserting the new signal path; added test_other_bad_request_still_fails covering the non-overflow 400. Total: 66 chat + refresh + main_window startup tests green; ruff clean on changed files. The 14 pre-existing UI suite failures (sessions/conversations refactor in flight elsewhere) were verified to reproduce on a clean checkout with PI-106 stashed, confirming they are unrelated.

6. Branch hygiene complication. The first commit attempt landed locally on a branch named pi-106-chat-slice-d-followups created off the local main, which carried an 'abandoned commit' (30d8088) — PI-076 db-export, design-tokens, and migration 0024 junk — ahead of origin/main. A concurrent session in the same Dropbox-synced clone then reset that branch and pushed PI-076/PI-105 commits to it on the remote, orphaning the PI-106 commit (a7fa813). The first 'git push' reported 'Everything up-to-date' because the local ref had already been moved before the push ran. Recovery: pinned a7fa813 to a new local branch pi-106-followups; rebased it with 'git rebase --onto origin/main 30d8088 pi-106-followups' inside an isolated worktree (crmbuilder-pi106, matching the per-PI worktree pattern) so the main clone's working tree and the concurrent session were never disturbed; verified the rebased branch contains exactly the 10 chat files with no migration / db-export / design-token junk; pushed pi-106-followups to origin as a new remote branch (9a98167). Doug subsequently merged it to main as 09ce4af. The mislabeled remote branch pi-106-chat-slice-d-followups was left untouched per Doug's direction.

Not done (deliberately): no interactive GUI smoke test from this headless session — the desktop app requires a display, the storage server, and a real Anthropic API key; the wiring is covered by Qt-level tests and the MainWindow startup test, and the interactive verification is left for Doug. No new DEC: the design (PI-052 §10 and §12) already settled the approach; PI-106 is polish on an existing surface, not a new architectural commitment.

Resolves PI-106.

(1) Threaded the existing RefreshService into ChatPanel; the controller tracks read entity types and the panel shows a header informational badge when one changes elsewhere — never re-calling. (2) Routed context-window 400s to a dedicated context_overflow signal so the panel surfaces an interactive 'Trim oldest messages & retry'; the trim helper drops the oldest ~third of turns while preserving Messages-API validity (begins on a fresh user turn, never orphans a tool_result). No DEC — design (PI-052 §10/§12) had already settled the approach.

session_description:
Single Claude Code execution at Doug's terminal. CNV-027. Parent workstream WS-011 (V2 storage API refinements). Medium: chat (claude_code runner).

Seed: "when trying to access a work ticket in V2, it failed to display when clicked on, and this message was displayed in the console ... Connection lost during refresh: [Errno 111] Connection refused". Doug then asked to look at two areas: (1) why the API process went offline, and (2) how to make the UI reporting more informative, including trying to resolve the issue.

Diagnosis. The dead instance was an externally-launched, detached crmbuilder-v2-api (PPID systemd --user) that produced no logs (stdout went to a closed terminal); journald/dmesg showed no OOM/signal, so the exact cause was unrecoverable. Two structural gaps: (a) no persistent API logs, so post-mortem was impossible; (b) the UI's ServerLifecycle only crash-monitors API subprocesses it spawns itself (QProcess) — an externally-run API is marked 'external' and is noticed dead only reactively, on the next failed request (the Connection refused the panel hit).

Decision (DEC-333). Asked Doug to choose the self-heal scope; he chose auto-restart + rotating logs + actionable banner over logs-only and over additionally adding a /health heartbeat (the heartbeat deferred to PI-111).

Delivered in one commit (9f8ec27, merged to main via PR #3 as 07b9313):
1. Rotating API logs at crmbuilder-v2/data/logs/api.log (config.api_log_path, gitignored). cli._build_api_log_config deep-copies uvicorn's LOGGING_CONFIG and adds a RotatingFileHandler (2 MB x 5) on the root and uvicorn/uvicorn.access loggers, passed as uvicorn.run(..., log_config=...). Built just before uvicorn.run so --check-only / fail-loud create no log file. Captures app logs, startup tracebacks, and access logs for both standalone and UI-spawned launches.
2. UI auto-restart in MainWindow: a panel connection_lost or an owned-subprocess crashed drives ServerLifecycle.start() up to _MAX_RECONNECT_ATTEMPTS (3), then a manual-Reconnect fallback banner. Banners name the URL, attempt count, log path, and standalone-launch hint; overlapping triggers dedupe; a runtime spawn failure (post first-ready, gated on had_first_ready()) routes to the in-window banner via app.py instead of the fatal startup dialog, so a failed reconnect never kills a live session. The _on_lifecycle_ready hide is now unconditional (more correct, deterministic).
